perQueryEvaluations.query: Within the 'Paris Saint-Germain FC vs. Liverpool FC' event, what are the current implied probabilities, best bids, best asks, and spreads for 'Will Paris Saint-Germain FC win on 2026-04-08?', 'Will Paris Saint-Germain FC vs. Liverpool FC end in a draw?', and 'Will Liverpool FC win on 2026-04-08?'?
quotedExcerpts: Please note that for the Draw and Liverpool FC win outcomes, specific market data ... was not returned by the source.
Draw | N/A ... Liverpool FC win | N/A

scoreExplanations.actionability: A trader gets one usable row, but cannot actually compare the full three-way match market.
scoreExplanations.dataFreshness: The answer is clearly framed as a same-session April 9 snapshot.
scoreExplanations.specificity: The PSG leg is numerically specific and the missing legs are called out explicitly.
scoreExplanations.uniqueness: Live quote detail on one event contract is still better than generic sports commentary, but the premium value is limited.
scoreExplanations.completeness: Two of the three requested outcomes are still unavailable, so the comparison remains materially incomplete.
scoreExplanations.dataAccuracy: Unlike the earlier mislabeled version, this rerun keeps the unresolved legs as unavailable, which matches the trace.

traceAssessment.selectionIssue: The first phrasing could not be matched, and the recovery path still relied on the same comparison endpoint without a stronger fallback for the missing legs.
traceAssessment.executionIssue: The run safely avoided relabeling errors, but it never recovered Draw and Liverpool quotes.
traceAssessment.completenessIssue: The buyer asked for a three-outcome quote table and only one outcome resolved with numbers.
traceAssessment.codeIssueDetail: The program converted unmatched outcomes into null rows instead of adding a targeted discovery step for the remaining sides.
traceAssessment.rootCauseEvidence: The final execution result contains one matched outcome (`Paris Saint-Germain FC`) and lists `Draw` and `Liverpool FC` in `unmatchedOutcomes`.
traceAssessment.responseQualityNote: Safer than the earlier bad mapping, but still not enough for a buyer who asked for the full match-state comparison.
traceAssessment.improvementLever: When two requested legs remain unmatched, follow the comparison call with per-outcome discovery rather than ending on a partially filled table.

diagnosticBrief.toolShouldHaveUsed: compare_event_outcome_quotes plus targeted follow-up resolution for unmatched legs
diagnosticBrief.argsUsed: {"eventQuery":"Paris Saint-Germain FC vs. Liverpool FC","outcomes":["Paris Saint-Germain FC","Draw","Liverpool FC"],"includeHistory":false}
diagnosticBrief.argsShouldHaveUsed: Same event comparison, then explicit follow-up discovery for `Draw` and `Liverpool FC` when they remained unmatched.
diagnosticBrief.codeSnippet: const unmatchedRows = unmatchedOutcomes.map((name) => ({ requestedName: name, matchedName: null, ... }));
diagnosticBrief.toolResultSample: PSG matched with 99.9c / 100.0c quotes; unmatchedOutcomes = ["Draw", "Liverpool FC"]
diagnosticBrief.completenessVerdict: Only one of the three requested rows resolved.
diagnosticBrief.stageTimingBottleneck: Three total attempts were spent on outcome matching.

perQueryEvaluations.overallVerdict: Mixed rerun: it no longer invents the missing rows, but it still fails the buyer's requested three-way comparison.

perQueryEvaluations.query: Within the '2026 FIFA World Cup Winner ' event, which sampled outcome has the strongest whale backing right now among Spain, France, England, Switzerland, New Zealand, and South Korea?
quotedExcerpts: Spain has the strongest whale backing among the teams you listed
The tool results did not include specific whale data for Switzerland, New Zealand, or South Korea, suggesting they currently rank lower

scoreExplanations.actionability: The buyer does get a named winner and real whale-value figures, but the answer is not trustworthy enough for the full six-name comparison.
scoreExplanations.dataFreshness: The whale data is clearly a same-session April 9 pull.
scoreExplanations.specificity: Spain, England, and France are backed by concrete whale counts, whale values, and prices.
scoreExplanations.uniqueness: Whale-backing detail is real premium venue-native signal.
scoreExplanations.completeness: Three of the six requested names were never actually surfaced with whale metrics, so the shortlist comparison is unfinished.
scoreExplanations.dataAccuracy: Spain may still be the right winner, but the response overstates confidence by implying omitted names rank lower when the trace only analyzed six of sixty total markets.

traceAssessment.selectionIssue: The whale method used a bounded top-volume scan rather than a forced check of the exact six names in the user prompt.
traceAssessment.executionIssue: The response treated omitted requested teams as lower-ranked instead of explicitly saying they were outside the analyzed subset.
traceAssessment.completenessIssue: Switzerland, New Zealand, and South Korea were not directly measured in the returned whale table.
traceAssessment.codeIssueDetail: The code filtered `whalesByOutcome` after a `maxOutcomes: 20` top-volume event scan, which does not guarantee coverage of all requested teams.
traceAssessment.dataAccuracyRedFlags: Trace note: `Analyzed 6 of 60 markets`, yet the final answer infers the unreturned requested teams simply rank lower.
traceAssessment.rootCauseEvidence: The tool result only returned six analyzed outcomes total, and the requested omitted teams never appeared in `whalesByOutcome`.
traceAssessment.responseQualityNote: Promising whale data, but not enough to conclusively answer the exact buyer shortlist.
traceAssessment.improvementLever: Use a bounded whale breakdown on the six named outcomes themselves instead of inferring from a top-volume event subset.

diagnosticBrief.toolShouldHaveUsed: A whale breakdown constrained to the six requested teams
diagnosticBrief.toolResultSample: Analyzed 6 of 60 markets; Spain $250,833.52, England $224,264.51, France $187,480.96; omitted requested teams absent from payload
diagnosticBrief.completenessVerdict: Partially answered, but not conclusive across the full requested set.
diagnosticBrief.stageTimingBottleneck: Coverage was bounded by the shortlist mode, not by runtime retries.

perQueryEvaluations.overallVerdict: Partially useful rerun: the surfaced whale numbers are strong, but the answer over-claims certainty on a six-team comparison it did not fully cover.

perQueryEvaluations.query: For the 'Will Paris Saint-Germain FC win on 2026-04-08?' market, what are the live trading parameters on the token, including tick size, fee rate, minimum order size, and whether negative risk is enabled?
quotedExcerpts: could not be deterministically resolved, so live trading parameters are currently unavailable
The following data points are required but were missing ... conditionId and tokenId
To get these results, you may want to try searching for a broader time window

scoreExplanations.actionability: The buyer gets no actual trading parameters, only a failure message and a search suggestion.
scoreExplanations.dataFreshness: The run did surface live discovery data from April 9, even though the requested parameter fetch never happened.
scoreExplanations.specificity: It is specific about which fields are missing, but not about the actual market settings the buyer asked for.
scoreExplanations.uniqueness: There is limited paid value because the premium answer ends at failure despite having venue-native identifiers available in trace.
scoreExplanations.completeness: Tick size, fee rate, minimum order size, and negative-risk status are all absent.
scoreExplanations.dataAccuracy: The response says `conditionId` and `tokenId` were missing, but the sports search trace shows an exact PSG match with both identifiers present.

traceAssessment.selectionIssue: The first search even resolved to the wrong event, then the corrected sports search still failed to use the exact match already in hand.
traceAssessment.executionIssue: The run stopped before calling `get_market_parameters` despite an exact PSG sports result with `primaryTokenId` and `primaryConditionId`.
traceAssessment.completenessIssue: None of the requested token-parameter fields were returned.
traceAssessment.codeIssueDetail: The program continued semantic targeting instead of using the exact `primaryOutcome` / `primaryTokenId` returned by discovery.
traceAssessment.dataAccuracyRedFlags: Trace shows `matchConfidence: exact`, `primaryTokenId`, and `primaryConditionId`, while the final answer says those identifiers were missing.
traceAssessment.rootCauseEvidence: The sports-only `search_and_get_outcomes` result contains one exact PSG outcome with token and condition IDs, but `executionResult` still returns `missingData` and stops.
traceAssessment.responseQualityNote: Honest about the miss, but not buyer-safe because the trace already had enough to continue.
traceAssessment.improvementLever: When discovery returns a single exact candidate with token and condition IDs, skip semantic reranking and call `get_market_parameters` directly.

diagnosticBrief.toolResultSample: eventTitle `Paris Saint-Germain FC vs. Liverpool FC`; totalOutcomes 1; primaryTokenId present; primaryConditionId present; matchConfidence `exact`
diagnosticBrief.completenessVerdict: Failed the parameter lookup despite having the identifiers.
diagnosticBrief.stageTimingBottleneck: Resolution logic stopped the run before the parameter tool was called.

perQueryEvaluations.overallVerdict: Weak rerun: the buyer gets a refusal even though the trace already found the exact PSG contract identifiers needed to continue.

perQueryEvaluations.query: For the 'Will Stephen A. Smith win the 2028 Democratic presidential nomination?' market, does the contract look rich or cheap relative to Gretchen Whitmer and Oprah Winfrey in the same 'Democratic Presidential Nominee 2028' event right now?
quotedExcerpts: Stephen A. Smith is currently priced between Gretchen Whitmer and Oprah Winfrey
Stephen A. Smith ($0.011) is priced at a 15.8% premium to Oprah Winfrey ($0.0095) but at a 12% discount to Gretchen Whitmer ($0.0125)
Stephen A. Smith market has the widest spread of the three (0.20c)

scoreExplanations.actionability: A buyer gets a usable relative-pricing read plus a meaningful liquidity caveat on Stephen's wider spread.
scoreExplanations.dataFreshness: The comparison is clearly tied to live April 9 quotes and volumes.
scoreExplanations.specificity: It provides exact mids, bid/ask ranges, spreads, and a quantified premium/discount framing.
scoreExplanations.uniqueness: This is real venue-native contract comparison rather than generic election speculation.
scoreExplanations.completeness: It answers the peer comparison well, though it could have gone one step further and label Stephen as roughly fair versus the average of the two peers.
scoreExplanations.dataAccuracy: The pairwise pricing and spread statements line up cleanly with the trace values.

traceAssessment.completenessIssue: The trace's local average-peer read was `roughly_fair`, which the response could have summarized more explicitly.
traceAssessment.responseQualityNote: Strong buyer-facing comparison with concrete quote detail and a legitimate liquidity note.
traceAssessment.improvementLever: Keep the exact three-leg quote table and add a one-line overall verdict such as `roughly fair versus peer average` when appropriate.

diagnosticBrief.argsUsed: {"eventQuery":"Democratic Presidential Nominee 2028","outcomes":["Stephen A. Smith","Gretchen Whitmer","Oprah Winfrey"],"includeHistory":false}
diagnosticBrief.codeSnippet: relativeRead = `roughly_fair` when Stephen sat between the two peer prices
diagnosticBrief.toolResultSample: Stephen 0.011 with 1.0c / 1.2c quote; Whitmer 0.0125; Oprah 0.0095; Stephen has the widest spread at 0.20c
diagnosticBrief.stageTimingBottleneck: None; the comparison resolved cleanly on the first pass.

perQueryEvaluations.overallVerdict: Strong rerun: it gives a grounded relative-pricing answer with enough quote detail for a buyer to judge the trade.

perQueryEvaluations.query: Within the 'US x Iran ceasefire by...?' event, which future buckets are still live after the expired dates, and among the live buckets which one currently has the highest quoted probability and the most volume?
quotedExcerpts: there are several live buckets remaining following the expiration of earlier dates
The following table includes all currently live buckets within the event

scoreExplanations.actionability: A buyer cannot trust which buckets are actually still live, so the screen is not tradable.
scoreExplanations.dataFreshness: The underlying event rows are current session data, even though they are misclassified.
scoreExplanations.specificity: The response is very numeric, but the key live-versus-expired classification is wrong.
scoreExplanations.uniqueness: There is some venue-native event data, but it is packaged in a buyer-misleading way.
scoreExplanations.completeness: The core request was to separate future live buckets from expired ones, and that separation failed.
scoreExplanations.dataAccuracy: The trace explicitly marks `April 7` as `active: false`, `closed: true`, and `dateStatus: expired`, yet the answer lists it as a live bucket and as the volume leader.

traceAssessment.selectionIssue: The workflow chose a single event-outcome fetch but then ignored the returned `active` and `dateStatus` fields.
traceAssessment.executionIssue: The code used a regex on the title instead of the explicit expiry fields already returned in the payload.
traceAssessment.completenessIssue: Expired March and April buckets were mixed into the supposedly live set, so the buyer never got a reliable future-bucket map.
traceAssessment.codeIssueDetail: The filter treated anything without `expired` in the title as live, even when the trace carried `active: false` and `dateStatus: expired`.
traceAssessment.dataAccuracyRedFlags: Trace row: `April 7` has `active: false`, `closed: true`, `endDate: 2026-04-07...`, `dateStatus: expired`, but the response still calls it live.
traceAssessment.rootCauseEvidence: The execution result note itself admits it did not independently verify live status or expired-date semantics beyond the available fields.
traceAssessment.responseQualityNote: Detailed, but buyer-unsafe because the main classification is wrong.
traceAssessment.improvementLever: Trust explicit `active`, `closed`, and `dateStatus` fields for rolling date buckets instead of inferring status from the label text.

diagnosticBrief.toolShouldHaveUsed: Same tool result, but with filtering on explicit status fields
diagnosticBrief.argsShouldHaveUsed: Same event fetch, followed by `active === true` and `dateStatus !== "expired"` filtering before ranking.
diagnosticBrief.codeSnippet: const isExpiredByTitle = /expired|expired date|expired dates/i.test(title); const isLiveByStatus = !isExpiredByTitle;
diagnosticBrief.toolResultSample: April 7 returned with price 0.9985, volume 106381998.68, active false, closed true, dateStatus expired
diagnosticBrief.stageTimingBottleneck: No runtime bottleneck; the miss was in classification logic.

perQueryEvaluations.overallVerdict: Weak rerun: it surfaces plenty of event rows, but the live-versus-expired judgment is wrong in exactly the place the buyer needed precision.
